name: Turdidae
description: Thrushes are medium-sized songbirds found worldwide, known for their melodious songs, spotted juvenile plumage, and a diet of earthworms, insects, and berries.

children.name: Turdus
children.description: The true thrushes — medium-sized songbirds with spotted or plain underparts, a rich melodic song, and a taste for earthworms and berries. Turdus is one of the most successful and widespread bird genera on Earth.

children.name: Turdus merula
children.description: The Common Blackbird is the most familiar garden bird across much of Europe, renowned for its fluty, unhurried song delivered from rooftops and treetops at dusk.

children.name: Turdus torquatus
children.description: The Ring Ouzel is a mountain blackbird with a distinctive white crescent on the breast, breeding in upland areas of Europe and wintering in North Africa.

children.name: Turdus philomelos
children.description: The Song Thrush is known for its rich, repetitive song and its habit of smashing snail shells against a stone 'anvil'.

children.name: Turdus viscivorus
children.description: The Mistle Thrush is a large, pale thrush with a bold, wild-sounding song delivered from high treetops, often in stormy weather.

children.name: Turdus pilaris
children.description: The Fieldfare is a colourful, gregarious thrush that breeds in northern Europe and Asia, wintering in large flocks that strip berry bushes.

children.name: Turdus iliacus
children.description: The Redwing is a small thrush with a distinctive creamy eyebrow and red flanks, breeding in Scandinavia and wintering across Europe.

children.name: Turdus ruficollis
children.description: A striking Asian thrush with a red throat and breast, breeding in Siberia and Central Asia, wintering in the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Turdus naumanni
children.description: Naumann's Thrush is an East Asian thrush with bold black scaling on white underparts, named after the German naturalist Johann Andreas Naumann.

children.name: Turdus obscurus
children.description: The Eyebrowed Thrush is a grey-headed Asian thrush with a bold white eyebrow, breeding in Siberia and migrating to Southeast Asia.

children.name: Turdus pallidus
children.description: The Pale Thrush is a plain brownish-grey thrush of East Asia, breeding in eastern Russia and wintering in Japan, Korea, and eastern China.

children.name: Turdus chrysolaus
children.description: The Brown-headed Thrush is a migratory Asian thrush with a rich brown head, breeding in Sakhalin and Japan and wintering in the Philippines and Taiwan.

children.name: Turdus cardis
children.description: The Japanese Thrush is a black-and-white thrush breeding in Japan and eastern China, wintering in southern China and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Turdus hortulorum
children.description: The Grey-backed Thrush is a delicate grey-and-rufous thrush of East Asian forests, breeding in eastern Russia and China, wintering in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Turdus celaenops
children.description: The Izu Thrush is an endemic of the Izu and Ryukyu Islands of Japan, with a black head, orange breast, and white belly. It is classified as Vulnerable due to its restricted island range.

children.name: Turdus migratorius
children.description: The American Robin is one of North America's most familiar birds, recognized by its brick-red breast and cheerful caroling song.

children.name: Turdus grayi
children.description: The national bird of Costa Rica, a plain brownish thrush common in gardens and urban areas from Mexico to northern Colombia.

children.name: Turdus rufiventris
children.description: The national bird of Brazil, with a vivid orange-red belly contrasting with a grey-brown back, common in forests and gardens across eastern and southern South America.

children.name: Turdus leucomelas
children.description: A widespread South American thrush with a pale, lightly streaked breast and bold yellow bill, found from Venezuela south to Argentina.

children.name: Turdus atrogularis
children.description: A striking Asian thrush with a solid black throat and upper breast contrasting with white underparts, a regular vagrant to western Europe.

children.name: Turdus eunomus
children.description: The Dusky Thrush is a boldly patterned East Asian thrush with heavy black scaling on white underparts, breeding in north-eastern Siberia.

children.name: Turdus kessleri
children.description: A large, striking thrush of high-altitude Tibetan scrub, with males boldly patterned in black, white, and chestnut.

children.name: Turdus rubrocanus
children.description: A large thrush of Himalayan and Chinese mountain forests with deep chestnut upperparts contrasting with a dark grey head.

children.name: Turdus boulboul
children.description: An elegant Himalayan thrush, with the male jet black with a bold pale grey wing panel and bright orange-yellow bill.

children.name: Turdus unicolor
children.description: A plain, pale-grey Asian thrush of forest and scrub across the Himalayas and Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Turdus dissimilis
children.description: A distinctive Himalayan thrush with a black head, throat, and breast contrasting with white and rufous underparts.

children.name: Turdus feae
children.description: A rare thrush breeding only in a restricted area of Myanmar and adjacent China, listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Turdus albicollis
children.description: A wide-ranging South American forest thrush with a clean white throat and upper breast contrasting with dark brown upperparts.

children.name: Turdus amaurochalinus
children.description: A common open-country thrush of southern South America with a pale creamy-white belly and lightly streaked throat.

children.name: Turdus chiguanco
children.description: A large, uniformly dark thrush of Andean valleys and towns from Ecuador to Argentina.

children.name: Turdus serranus
children.description: A sleek, entirely black thrush of Andean cloud forests with a bright orange-yellow bill and eye-ring in the male.

children.name: Turdus nigriceps
children.description: A subtly plumaged thrush of Andean forests in Peru, Bolivia, and Argentina, with slaty-grey upperparts and a pale, streaked throat.

children.name: Turdus subalaris
children.description: A thrush of south-eastern South American forests, with slaty-grey upperparts, a whitish throat, and a yellow bill. Formerly considered conspecific with the Andean Slaty Thrush.

children.name: Turdus fuscater
children.description: The largest South American thrush, a dark sooty-brown bird with a vivid orange bill, common in Andean cloud forests and parks from Venezuela to Bolivia.

children.name: Turdus olivaceus
children.description: The most widespread thrush of sub-Saharan Africa, with an orange-yellow bill and orange-buff flanks, common in gardens and highland parks.

children.name: Turdus abyssinicus
children.description: A familiar orange-billed thrush widespread across sub-Saharan Africa, common in forest edge, gallery forest, and large gardens.

children.name: Turdus pelios
children.description: A plain grey-brown thrush with a yellowish bill found across West and Central African forests and woodland edges.

children.name: Turdus helleri
children.description: A critically endangered thrush restricted to tiny forest fragments in the Taita Hills of south-east Kenya.

children.name: Turdus menachensis
children.description: A boldly marked thrush endemic to montane forests of south-west Saudi Arabia and Yemen, listed as Near Threatened.

children.name: Turdus plumbeus
children.description: A boldly patterned Caribbean thrush with striking red legs, eye-ring, and bill base, found across the Greater Antilles and Bahamas.

children.name: Turdus jamaicensis
children.description: A Jamaican endemic with a distinctive white iris that becomes more prominent with age.

children.name: Turdus fumigatus
children.description: A warm, richly brown thrush of forest interior from Trinidad and the Lesser Antilles south through the Amazon basin.

children.name: Turdus nudigenis
children.description: A pale, sandy-brown thrush with large, conspicuous yellow orbital rings, ranging from the Lesser Antilles through Venezuela and Colombia.

children.name: Turdus poliocephalus
children.description: One of the most polytypic birds in the world, with over 50 subspecies ranging across Pacific and Indian Ocean islands.

children.name: Turdus swalesi
children.description: A rare Caribbean thrush restricted to montane forests of Hispaniola, listed as Endangered.

children.name: Turdus simillimus
children.description: A thrush of the Western Ghats and Sri Lankan hills, formerly considered a subspecies of the Common Blackbird.

children.name: Turdus maximus
children.description: A large, dark thrush of the high Tibetan plateau, found in juniper and willow scrub above 3,500 m.

children.name: Turdus olivaceofuscus
children.description: A thrush endemic to São Tomé and Príncipe in the Gulf of Guinea.

children.name: Turdus lherminieri
children.description: A thrush of the Lesser Antilles, found in dense montane forest understorey with a beautiful, flute-like song.

children.name: Turdus assimilis
children.description: A thrush of Central and South American highlands with a distinctive white throat patch.

children.name: Turdus lawrencii
children.description: A thrush of the upper Amazon basin with a clear, bell-like song that is distinctive of the Amazonian dawn chorus.

children.name: Turdus hauxwelli
children.description: A thrush of the western Amazon basin named after the English collector John Hauxwell.

children.name: Turdus maculirostris
children.description: A thrush of the Tumbesian region of western Ecuador and northwestern Peru, with a spotted bill.

children.name: Turdus plebejus
children.description: A thrush of Central American highlands, from southern Mexico to Panama.

children.name: Turdus aurantius
children.description: A thrush of Jamaica, the Cayman Islands, and Cuba, with a white chin and orange-yellow bill and legs.

children.name: Turdus rufitorques
children.description: A thrush of Central American highlands from southern Mexico to El Salvador, with a distinctive rufous collar across the upper breast.

children.name: Turdus rufopalliatus
children.description: A thrush endemic to western Mexico, with a grey head, rufous back, and white belly with rufous flanks.

children.name: Turdus infuscatus
children.description: A dark thrush of highland forests from Mexico to El Salvador, with the male almost entirely black with an orange bill.

children.name: Turdus fulviventris
children.description: A thrush of the Venezuelan and Colombian Andes, with a chestnut belly, grey head, and olive-brown back.

children.name: Turdus obsoletus
children.description: A thrush of the Chocó region in Colombia and Ecuador, with olive-brown upperparts and a pale vent.

children.name: Turdus ignobilis
children.description: A thrush of Amazonian and Andean foothill forests, with a black bill, olive-brown upperparts, and white underparts.

children.name: Turdus leucops
children.description: A thrush of the Andean cloud forests, with a distinctive pale eye, dark plumage, and orange bill.

children.name: Turdus maranonicus
children.description: A thrush restricted to the dry Marañón Valley of northern Peru, with grey-brown upperparts and whitish underparts.

children.name: Turdus haplochrous
children.description: A uniform greyish thrush endemic to the Bolivian Andes, with little variation in plumage across its body.

children.name: Turdus sanchezorum
children.description: A recently described thrush from white-sand forests of the western Amazon, separated from the Black-billed Thrush on genetic grounds.

children.name: Turdus albocinctus
children.description: A large blackbird of the Himalayas, with a striking white collar across the upper breast. It inhabits moist temperate forests from Pakistan to Bhutan.

children.name: Turdus anthracinus
children.description: A dark, uniformly sooty-black thrush of montane forests in the Himalayas from Nepal to northern Myanmar.

children.name: Turdus reevei
children.description: A thrush of the Tumbesian region of Ecuador and Peru, with grey upperparts, white underparts, and a yellow bill and legs.

children.name: Turdus olivater
children.description: A thrush of tepui highlands and Andean foothills, with a black hood, olive-brown back, and white breast.

children.name: Turdus falcklandii
children.description: A thrush of southern South America and the Falkland Islands, with olive-brown upperparts, a spotted breast, and a yellow bill.

children.name: Turdus nigrescens
children.description: A sooty-black thrush of highland forests in Costa Rica and western Panama, with a bright orange bill and eye-ring.

children.name: Turdus chrysogenys
children.description: A thrush of the Brazilian Atlantic Forest, with a yellow eye-ring, olive-brown upperparts, and pale underparts.

children.name: Turdus fumosus
children.description: A thrush of the Guianas and northern Brazil, found in lowland forest and cocoa plantations.

children.name: Turdus chrysolus
children.description: A thrush of the Solomon Islands and Vanuatu with a yellow throat patch.

children.name: Turdus mendanae
children.description: A thrush of the Solomon Islands, formerly considered conspecific with the Island Thrush.

children.name: Turdus replicatus
children.description: A recently identified species from the Chocó region of Colombia and Ecuador, with a two-part song.

children.name: Turdus faeae
children.description: A thrush of São Tomé island in the Gulf of Guinea, one of the least known African thrushes.

children.name: Turdus libonyana
children.description: A thrush of southern African savannas and woodlands, with a rufous belly and white vent.

children.name: Turdus tephronotus
children.description: A thrush of the Ethiopian highlands, with a grey breast and brown back.

children.name: Turdus smithi
children.description: A thrush of the Karoo region of South Africa, separated from the Olive Thrush on genetic grounds.

children.name: Turdus macrocus
children.description: A thrush of the Guianas and northern Brazil with a notably large bill.

children.name: Turdus fasciatus
children.description: A thrush of the Bolivian and Peruvian Andes with banded underparts.

children.name: Geokichla
children.description: Ground thrushes with bold spotted or scaled underparts, typically inhabitants of dense forest undergrowth.

children.name: Geokichla sibirica
children.description: A spectacular long-distance migrant that breeds in Siberian taiga and winters in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Geokichla citrina
children.description: A strikingly beautiful thrush with a vivid orange head and underparts, found in forests across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Geokichla piaggiae
children.description: A secretive forest-floor thrush of East African montane forests.

children.name: Geokichla spiloptera
children.description: A boldly spotted forest thrush endemic to Sri Lanka, listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Geokichla interpres
children.description: A small, secretive forest-floor thrush with a bold chestnut crown, found from Myanmar to Borneo.

children.name: Geokichla lunulata
children.description: An Australian forest thrush with intricate crescent-shaped markings across its underparts.

children.name: Geokichla heinei
children.description: An Australian rainforest thrush, distinguished from the Bassian Thrush by its plain rufous-brown upperparts.

children.name: Geokichla gurneyi
children.description: An African forest-floor thrush with vivid orange face and underparts, found from South Africa to Tanzania.

children.name: Zoothera
children.description: Large, heavily scaled thrushes of forests and woodland, with bold crescent-scaled patterns unique among thrushes.

children.name: Zoothera aurea
children.description: The largest thrush in Europe, a rare vagrant from Siberia with a bold crescent-scaled pattern.

children.name: Zoothera mollissima
children.description: A high-altitude thrush of the Himalayas and Tibetan plateau.

children.name: Zoothera dauma
children.description: A large, boldly patterned thrush covered in black-edged golden-buff scales.

children.name: Zoothera andromedae
children.description: A boldly scaled forest thrush of the Greater Sunda Islands.

children.name: Zoothera marginata
children.description: A dusky, long-billed thrush of dense Himalayan undergrowth.

children.name: Zoothera monticola
children.description: A large, dark brown thrush with a prominently long tail found in the eastern Himalayas.

children.name: Zoothera crossleyi
children.description: A secretive African forest-floor thrush of montane forests in Cameroon and the eastern DRC.

children.name: Catharus
children.description: Spotted thrushes of North and Central America, renowned for some of the most beautiful songs of any bird.

children.name: Catharus guttatus
children.description: Widely regarded as producing one of the finest bird songs in the world — a series of ethereal, ascending flute-like spirals.

children.name: Catharus ustulatus
children.description: A long-distance migrant with a spiraling, upward-winding song, hallmark of northern forest evenings.

children.name: Catharus fuscescens
children.description: A warm russet-brown thrush known for its cascading, downward-spiraling song.

children.name: Catharus minimus
children.description: A long-distance boreal migrant with a pale grey face, breeding in spruce-fir forests across Alaska and Canada.

children.name: Catharus bicknelli
children.description: A small, secretive thrush breeding only on the highest peaks of north-eastern North America, listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Catharus aurantiirostris
children.description: A retiring forest-understorey thrush with a vivid orange bill, legs, and eye-ring, found from Mexico to Venezuela.

children.name: Catharus dryas
children.description: A boldly spotted nightingale-thrush with an orange bill, found in dense undergrowth from Mexico to South America.

children.name: Catharus frantzii
children.description: A thrush of highland forests from Mexico to Panama, with a chestnut cap and grey sides.

children.name: Catharus gracilipes
children.description: A thrush of Andean cloud forests from Venezuela to Bolivia, with dark grey plumage and a sweet, five-note song.

children.name: Catharus occidentalis
children.description: A thrush of Mexican pine-oak and cloud forests, with a russet-brown back and a sweet, melodious song.

children.name: Catharus fuscater
children.description: A large, dark nightingale-thrush of Andean cloud forests from Costa Rica to Bolivia, with a rich, fluty song.

children.name: Catharus mexicanus
children.description: A thrush of lowland and foothill forests from Mexico to Panama, with a blackish head and a pale throat.

children.name: Catharus gracilirostris
children.description: A small nightingale-thrush of highland forests from Costa Rica to western Panama, with a black bill and a simple, whistled song.

children.name: Hylocichla
children.description: A monotypic genus containing only the Wood Thrush, one of the most celebrated songbirds of eastern North America.

children.name: Hylocichla mustelina
children.description: A plump, richly spotted thrush celebrated for its ethereal, flute-like song. Listed as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Sialia
children.description: Bluebirds — small thrushes of North and Central America with brilliant blue or blue-and-chestnut plumage.

children.name: Sialia sialis
children.description: A beloved thrush with vivid blue upperparts and a rich chestnut-red breast, rebounded thanks to nest box programs.

children.name: Sialia currucoides
children.description: An ethereal sky-blue thrush of western North American mountain meadows, lacking the chestnut breast of its relatives.

children.name: Sialia mexicana
children.description: A stunning thrush of open pine and oak woodlands in western North America, with deep blue upperparts and chestnut breast.

children.name: Ixoreus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Varied Thrush, a striking Pacific coast thrush with bold orange and black patterning.

children.name: Ixoreus naevius
children.description: A stunning Pacific coast thrush with orange eyebrow stripes, wingbars, and breast contrasting with blue-grey upperparts.

children.name: Myadestes
children.description: Solitaires — slender, long-tailed thrushes of montane forests in the Americas and Hawaii, known for extraordinarily melodious songs.

children.name: Myadestes townsendi
children.description: A slender, grey thrush of western North American coniferous forests, a territorial winter frugivore that defends juniper berry patches.

children.name: Myadestes occidentalis
children.description: A solitaire of highland forests from Mexico to Honduras, with a brown back, grey head and breast, and a rich, fluty song.

children.name: Myadestes elisabeth
children.description: A slender grey thrush endemic to Cuba, with an extraordinary, complex song considered one of the finest in the Caribbean.

children.name: Myadestes genibarbis
children.description: A distinctive solitaire of Lesser Antilles montane forests, with a warm rufous throat and a haunting, pure whistled song.

children.name: Myadestes melanops
children.description: A stunning Central American solitaire with a velvety black face mask contrasting with blue-grey plumage and an orange bill.

children.name: Myadestes coloratus
children.description: A solitaire of the Talamancan mountains of Costa Rica and Panama, with grey upperparts, rufous underparts, and a varied, melodic song.

children.name: Myadestes ralloides
children.description: A slender, grey thrush of Andean cloud forests with an extraordinary song of rich, varied flute-like phrases.

children.name: Myadestes obscurus
children.description: A plain, dark brown solitaire endemic to the Big Island of Hawaii, the only native thrush still relatively common in Hawaii.

children.name: Cichlopsis
children.description: A monotypic genus of South American solitaires with a plain, uniformly brown plumage and a rich, melodious song.

children.name: Cichlopsis leucogenys
children.description: A warm rufous-brown solitaire found in the tepui highlands and Andean foothills from Venezuela to Brazil and Peru. Its song is a haunting series of clear, descending whistles.

children.name: Entomodestes
children.description: A small genus of South American solitaires with striking black-and-white or entirely black plumage, found in cloud forests of the Andes.

children.name: Entomodestes leucotis
children.description: A striking solitaire of Andean cloud forests from Peru to Bolivia, with a white ear patch contrasting with otherwise black plumage and a pale bill.

children.name: Entomodestes coracinus
children.description: An entirely black solitaire of cloud forests in Colombia and Ecuador, with a black bill and legs. Its song is a rich, fluty series of phrases.

children.name: Platycichla
children.description: A small South American genus of thrushes closely related to Turdus but with a flatter bill profile.

children.name: Platycichla flavipes
children.description: The male is glossy blue-black with yellow legs, bill, and eye-ring; females are brown above and streaked below. Found from Trinidad and Venezuela south through eastern Brazil.

children.name: Platycichla leucops
children.description: A sleek, all-black thrush with a strikingly white or cream iris, found on Andean slopes and tepuis from Colombia to Peru.

children.name: Psophocichla
children.description: A monotypic African genus containing the Groundscraper Thrush, a large, boldly spotted thrush of open dry savanna.

children.name: Psophocichla litsitsirupa
children.description: A large, distinctive thrush with bold black spots on white underparts, found in open savanna and dry woodland from Ethiopia to South Africa.

children.name: Cochoa
children.description: A small genus of colourful, pitta-like thrushes found in montane forests of Southeast Asia, with striking purple or green plumage.

children.name: Cochoa purpurea
children.description: A stunning thrush of Himalayan and Southeast Asian montane forests, with the male a deep purple with a silvery cap.

children.name: Cochoa viridis
children.description: A brilliant green thrush with blue wings and tail, found in montane forests from the Himalayas to Indochina.

children.name: Cochoa azurea
children.description: A rare, azure-blue cochoa endemic to the montane forests of Java, classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Neocossyphus
children.description: A genus of African ant thrushes, closely related to true thrushes but with rufous tails and a habit of following driver ant swarms.

children.name: Neocossyphus rufus
children.description: A thrush of West and Central African forests, with a rufous tail and brown upperparts. It regularly follows driver ant swarms.

children.name: Neocossyphus poensis
children.description: A thrush with a white-tipped tail and a distinctive habit of following army ants in Central African forests.

children.name: Neocossyphus finschii
children.description: A little-known ant thrush of West African forests, named after the German naturalist Otto Finsch.

children.name: Stizorhina
children.description: A small genus of African thrushes, closely related to Neocossyphus, with rufous plumage and a distinctive habit of tail-flicking.

children.name: Stizorhina fraseri
children.description: A rufous-brown thrush of Central African forests, named after the British zoologist Louis Fraser.

children.name: Stizorhina insularis
children.description: A rufous thrush endemic to Bioko Island, often treated as a subspecies of Fraser's Rufous Thrush.

children.name: Chlamydochaera
children.description: A monotypic Bornean genus containing the Fruithunter, an unusual thrush that superficially resembles a miniature pitta.

children.name: Chlamydochaera jefferyi
children.description: A distinctive Bornean montane thrush with a black head, white underparts, and chestnut-orange back. Listed as Near Threatened.

children.name: Geomalia
children.description: A monotypic genus from Sulawesi containing a large, ground-haunting thrush of montane forest interior.

children.name: Geomalia heinrichi
children.description: A large, dark, long-legged thrush endemic to the montane forests of Sulawesi, classified as Vulnerable.
